task_yaml.task:
My director wants a cross-incident writeup that actually teaches us
something from six past outages, and I need help putting it together.
Use the systematic debugging skill to walk through each PM consistently,
the Markdown formatter to keep the output clean, and the memory-management skill
plus the daily digest skill to organize the notes you pull out into a
proper synthesis.

The six postmortems live under /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/:
pm_2022_q2_bgp_misconfig.md, pm_2019_q3_waf_regex.md,
pm_2017_q1_db_outage.md, pm_2025_q1_outage.md,
pm_2025_q2_db_migration.md, and pm_2025_q3_config_drift.md.

The whole point of this exercise is surfacing the cross-cutting story.
The director wants the full set of root-cause patterns that recur
across multiple incidents — every cross-cutting pattern these six PMs
actually support, nothing left out. They also want every incident
represented in the matrix with the concrete facts that make each one
distinguishable; citing filenames without real incident detail won't
cut it. On top of that, the writeup needs to carry threads on
detection gaps, response and comms breakdowns, prevention coverage
across all six, and a clear systemic-versus-one-off classification.
Every one of those threads must be visible somewhere in the
deliverables or the writeup is incomplete.

Merge everything into /tmp_workspace/results/insights.md with four
sections:
  - "Common Root Causes" — recurring causes across multiple incidents.
    I want every cross-incident pattern the six PMs support; do not
    stop at one or two and leave the rest unmentioned. Each pattern
    should be backed by three or more incident files with concrete
    source references into the postmortems.
  - "Incident Taxonomy Matrix" — one row per incident, all six
    represented, with trigger, blast radius, detection gap,
    rollback/recovery gap, and the most relevant prevention owner.
    Each row must mention the specific incident detail that
    distinguishes that PM, not a generic restatement.
  - "Prevention Checklist" — at least ten concrete preventative
    actions, each tagged with one of infra, db, release, monitoring,
    or process.
  - "Open Gaps" — at least four guardrails that are missing or only
    weakly covered across these postmortems but that we should
    introduce anyway. Tie each gap back to the incidents that
    motivate it; do not list a gap unless more than one incident
    supports it.

Also save /tmp_workspace/results/action_register.csv for the director's
follow-up tracker. Columns: action_id, prevention_action, domain,
owner, source_incidents, root_cause_addressed, dependency, due_window,
success_signal. Make it tracker-ready with at least ten concrete
actions. The register has to do real work, not duplicate the prose
checklist — at minimum it must carry one action that addresses each
cross-incident root-cause pattern from the synthesis, and one action
specifically motivated by each of the six source incidents. Every
action must trace back to at least one source incident filename and
name the cross-incident pattern it addresses.

The four-section skeleton plus the register is the structural floor —
you still need the broader cross-cutting threads (root-cause patterns,
detection-gap patterns, response and comms gaps, prevention coverage,
systemic versus one-off classification) to come through somewhere
across the two deliverables.
